opp.next_step: Send focused demo content for contract management supplier management and procurement and ask them to review internally with the partner Schedule next call with Demo including both decision makers to align on how they currently handle contracts suppliers and purchasing in May Deep dive into their current workflow and quantify volume processes and inefficiencies to anchor real use cases Close each interaction with a defined next step pushing toward a clear decision on adopting a structured operating layer
opp.ais_why:
• DWG's David Martin had to ask 'What is an RFI?' during the intro, putting the conversation at procurement-101 rather than AI maturity.
• Zycus introduced the Merlin intake chatbot and Teams integration, but the prospect's reaction focused on a broken supplier-portal profile link and basic CLM education.
• One 22-minute call with a 5-10 user deployment scope; too sparse and too early to read as anything but Resistant.

items.quote: No budget or EB discussion on 22 Apr call; David asked pricing, Grace deferred pending scope. Next_Step__c references 'both decision makers' but second DM not named.
David sole contact, one call, no follow-on engagement, no introductions, no advocacy evidence. Mentioned 'my partner' but no name or role.
No discussion of who else involved, approval steps, or timeline to decision. David said 'we're going at their speed' (re: Balfour Beatty) but his own decision process not mapped.
David stated 2-3 contracts/month, manual processes, but no cycle-time, cost, error-rate, or savings numbers captured.
Only David Martin engaged; business partner mentioned but not named, no email/contact, did not join call. No IT, finance, exec stakeholders identified.

sources: Avoma call 22 Apr 2026, David Martin: 'I would say maybe two to 3 depending on the size, mixed with other individual jobs'
Avoma call 22 Apr 2026, David: 'a lot of the times, these softwares, it turns into a lot more work using the software... I've used Slack and CRMs, but then you're just logging data'
Avoma notes: 'Uncertainty about how Zycus works in practice and its efficiency in reducing workload versus adding complexity' (concern)
Avoma notes: 'Need for automation that reduces manual work and speeds up procurement and contract processes due to no current tool usage'
metrics.narrative: David Martin stated on the 22 Apr 2026 call that he manages 2 to 3 contracts monthly, mixed with individual job-based work in construction/remodeling (6-month full remodels plus per-job contracts). He articulated qualitative pain around manual contract management with no current software and concern that tools often 'turn into a lot more work' rather than reducing it. No quantified financial impact, cycle-time, or ROI stated. Grace positioned AI-led contract authoring, clause-risk flagging, and executive summaries as time-savers but did not anchor to a specific cost or efficiency number David confirmed. The business problem is confirmed (manual, ad-hoc contract + procurement processes for a new business entity, DWGS, in construction supply); the quantified value case is not. Next move: on re-engagement, quantify current contract/procurement cycle time, error rate, and manual hours to build a savings model.

sources: Avoma call 22 Apr 2026: David Martin sole buyer participant, showed interest, requested videos
No follow-on call, no introductions, no internal advocacy evidence

champion.narrative: No champion identified. David Martin is the sole buyer contact; he participated in one discovery call (22 Apr 2026, 65 days ago) and showed interest ('quite intrigued'), requested demo videos and the call recording to share with his business partner, and said 'we'll continue on'. Grace committed to send videos and schedule a follow-up call. However, no logged activity or buyer reply since 7 May (50 days ago), and no next meeting scheduled. David has not introduced Grace to his partner, not provided internal advocacy evidence (no intro to an EB, no pushing Zycus internally, no shared docs), and the relationship is one call deep with no follow-on engagement. At this stage David is an engaged early contact, not a champion. His partner (unnamed, role unknown) is a potential co-evaluator but has not engaged. Champion development stalled due to 50-day silence. Next move: re-engage David, deliver on the video commitment if not yet sent, and secure the next call with both David and his partner to assess advocacy and access.

sources: Avoma call 22 Apr 2026, Grace: 'we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers... pretty established players in the US market'
No evidence David is evaluating GEP, Coupa, or JAGGAER
David: 'nothing at all' (no current software), exploratory engagement
Salesforce Competitors__c: unknown (field not populated)
competition.narrative: Competition confirmed from the 22 Apr 2026 call. Grace positioned Zycus against GEP, Coupa, and JAGGAER as 'pretty established players in the US market' that Zycus is 'on the same level as', framing them as the competitive peer set. David did not state he is evaluating any of these competitors, nor did he mention any active RFP or multi-vendor bake-off; this was Grace's competitive positioning in a discovery/education call. David's engagement is exploratory ('just curious', 'initial browsing for tools'), not a formal eval. No evidence David has engaged GEP, Coupa, or JAGGAER. The current alternative is do-nothing: David uses no software today ('nothing at all'), so the competitive dynamic is Zycus vs. status quo (manual processes). GEP, Coupa, JAGGAER are latent threats (the peer set a buyer in this space would likely encounter if they run a formal eval), not confirmed active competitors. How we win: demonstrate Zycus is intuitive and reduces workload (David's stated fear) rather than adding complexity, prove AI delivers efficiency gains on his 2-3 contracts/month use case, and convert exploratory interest into a scoped pilot or trial before a formal RFP brings in the peer set.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain confirmed: David Martin articulated on 22 Apr 2026 that he currently uses no software to manage business operations, contracts, procurement, or purchasing ('nothing at all'). He manages 2 to 3 contracts monthly plus individual job-based work in construction/remodeling (6-month full remodels, per-job contracts), all manually. He expressed concern that many software tools 'turn into a lot more work... just logging data and moving things' rather than reducing workload, citing experience with Slack and CRMs. He is 'not really sure what the point of Zycus is' (stated at call open) but became 'quite intrigued' after Grace's walkthrough, saying 'it does a lot... more importantly, how efficiently does it do it'. Pain owner: David Martin, Procurement Manager and business owner at DWGS. Pain is operational inefficiency and lack of structure in contract/supplier/procurement workflows for a new, growing business, with specific fear of software adding complexity rather than solving it. Grace positioned Zycus AI (clause-risk flagging, auto onboarding, Merlin chatbot) as efficiency-multipliers; David requested demo videos to validate. Pain is confirmed but not quantified (no cycle-time, cost, or error-rate numbers).

paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, procurement mechanics, InfoSec, SI involvement) entirely unknown. No discussion of MSA, SOW, legal review, security questionnaire, or IT approval on the 22 Apr call. David is a new business owner (started DWGS as a new entity for a business venture in construction supply); no mention of existing vendor-approval or procurement policies. No SI/partner-led implementation mentioned; David is a supplier/vendor to the relevant stakeholder (using Zycus as Balfour's vendor portal) but that relationship is not tied to this opportunity's paper process. No contract expiration forcing function. At this early stage (discovery, no pricing/proposal yet), paper-process discovery has not occurred. Next move: on re-engagement, confirm whether DWGS has procurement/legal review requirements for a SaaS purchase, and map the approval path.

decision_process.narrative: Decision process unknown. Only one discovery call occurred (22 Apr 2026), with one buyer participant (David Martin). No second stakeholder joined; no executive, finance, or IT presence. David mentioned a 'partner' (business partner, not an SI) he would share the recording and videos with, but that partner's name, role, and decision authority are unstated. Grace's Next_Step__c (1 Jun 2026) references 'both decision makers' for the next call, implying a two-person or committee decision, but the second DM is not named in any evidence. No approval chain, no procurement process, no timeline for decision stated. David characterized his engagement as exploratory ('just curious... not really sure what the point of Zycus is') and confirmed 'we're going at their speed' regarding his customer Balfour Beatty, but his own internal decision process for adopting Zycus was not discussed. No mention of legal, InfoSec, IT approvals, or contract review. The call ended with Grace committing to send videos and David saying 'we'll continue on', but no next meeting was scheduled on the call. Next move: on re-engagement, map the decision process — who else is involved, what steps/approvals are required, and timeline to decision.

sources: Avoma call 22 Apr 2026, David: 'Definitely, the contract management, the supplier risk and performance management, and the eProcurement... those stood out to me'
Avoma call 22 Apr 2026, David: 'how efficiently does it do it... a lot of the times, these softwares, it turns into a lot more work'
Avoma notes: 'User-friendly interface and efficient workflow to avoid adding workload, emphasizing ease of use and automation'
Avoma notes: 'Desire for AI features that flag contract risks early and provide executive summaries to save time and improve compliance'
Grace confirmed modular purchasing on the call
decision_criteria.narrative: David expressed interest in three specific modules on the 22 Apr call: iContract (contract management), iSupplier (supplier risk/performance), and eProcurement, stating 'those are right around my alley'. Within those, he highlighted ease of use, workflow efficiency, and AI-driven automation that reduces manual work rather than adding complexity as his primary evaluation lens. He specifically cited concern that software often becomes 'a lot more work... just logging data' and asked 'how efficiently does it do it'. Grace positioned Zycus on AI differentiators: clause-risk flagging, executive summaries, proactive vendor onboarding, Merlin Intake chatbot for intuitive procurement requests. David showed interest in the chatbot concept and requested demo videos to see the user interface in action. No formal RFP, scorecard, or structured criteria stated; no comparison grid or technical requirements. Criteria inferred from stated needs: (1) contract + supplier + procurement scope, (2) AI/automation that saves time, (3) intuitive UI that does not add workload, (4) modular/flexible (Grace confirmed modularity). Confirmed as initial browsing, not a formal eval. Next move: on re-engagement, validate these as his decision framework and confirm whether any formal eval or RFP will follow.

His stated need (efficiency, reduce manual work, avoid 'just logging data') aligns with AI-driven automation value prop. Products include Merlin + Agentic AI. However, no AIS score/status in Salesforce (fields null), so AI Hungry/Curious/Resistant tier cannot be authoritatively stated. From call evidence alone: AI Curious (interested, wants to see it in action, but not yet demanding AI or quantifying AI-specific ROI).

north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage deal remains stalled 50 days with no buyer response since May 7. Single discovery call held in April; rep sent demo videos but David Martin has gone dark. No champion, no urgency signals, inertia winning.

flags: Stalled post-commitment: Grace committed demo videos + call recording on 22 Apr call; no logged delivery or buyer reply since 7 May (50 days ago). Re-engage immediately: confirm videos were sent, offer live demo walk-through if videos did not land, and schedule next call.
best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal (one discovery call, 50-day stall) with critical gaps: single-threaded (only David Martin engaged, partner unnamed), no EB/budget validation, no champion, no quantified value case, and stalled post-commitment (Grace promised videos/follow-up, no buyer reply). Immediate priorities: re-engage David, deliver on commitments, secure next call with David + partner, and qualify EB/budget before investing further.

competitive_position.summary: Primary competition is do-nothing (status quo: David uses no software today). GEP, Coupa, and JAGGAER are latent peer threats named by Grace as the competitive set Zycus competes with in the US market, but no evidence David is evaluating them. David's engagement is exploratory, not a formal RFP. Threat ranking: (1) do-nothing / inertia (high — 50-day silence suggests low urgency), (2) GEP/Coupa/JAGGAER (medium — likely to surface if David runs a formal eval, but not active now). Win by proving value fast (pilot/trial), demonstrating ease-of-use and efficiency (David's stated criteria), and converting exploratory interest into commitment before a formal eval brings in the peer set.

competitors.name: Do-nothing / status quo
competitors.quote: Are you guys using any technology right now, any software to manage any business operations... or nothing at all? David: nothing at all.
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of manual contract/procurement processes (cycle time, errors, missed savings) and prove Zycus AI delivers fast ROI on David's 2-3 contracts/month use case; pilot or trial to create urgency and lock in before inertia wins.

competitors.name: GEP
competitors.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers. So not sure if you're as familiar with them, but they are pretty established players in the US market.
competitors.how_we_win: Position Zycus AI (Merlin, clause-risk flagging, exec summaries) as more intuitive and automation-forward than GEP; emphasize modular flexibility and faster time-to-value for a small/growing business like DWGS; avoid triggering a formal RFP that brings GEP in.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers.
competitors.how_we_win: Coupa typically enterprise-scale and expensive; position Zycus as better fit for mid-market/growing business with modular pricing and AI-first ease-of-use.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers.
competitors.how_we_win: JAGGAER peer; differentiate on AI (Merlin chatbot, contract AI) and user experience (David's stated priority).

value.name: David Martin
value.risk: Cooling: 50-day silence post-call; requested videos but no logged reply or follow-up scheduled.
value.title: Procurement Manager
value.source: Salesforce OpportunityContactRole (Decision Maker), Avoma call 22 Apr 2026

packets.subject: David Martin

value.name: Do-nothing / status quo
value.quote: Are you guys using any technology right now, any software to manage any business operations... or nothing at all? David: nothing at all.
value.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of manual contract/procurement processes (cycle time, errors, missed savings) and prove Zycus AI delivers fast ROI on David's 2-3 contracts/month use case; pilot or trial to create urgency and lock in before inertia wins.

packets.subject: Do-nothing / status quo

value.name: GEP
value.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers. So not sure if you're as familiar with them, but they are pretty established players in the US market.
value.how_we_win: Position Zycus AI (Merlin, clause-risk flagging, exec summaries) as more intuitive and automation-forward than GEP; emphasize modular flexibility and faster time-to-value for a small/growing business like DWGS; avoid triggering a formal RFP that brings GEP in.

packets.subject: GEP

value.name: Coupa
value.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers.
value.how_we_win: Coupa typically enterprise-scale and expensive; position Zycus as better fit for mid-market/growing business with modular pricing and AI-first ease-of-use.

packets.subject: Coupa

value.name: JAGGAER
value.quote: Grace: we're up there with the GEPs and the Coopas and the Jaggers.
value.how_we_win: JAGGAER peer; differentiate on AI (Merlin chatbot, contract AI) and user experience (David's stated priority).

packets.subject: JAGGAER
